coredata.dc:title: Ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal method to fabricate MoS2 quantum dots in aqueous solution as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ ion detection
coredata.dc:creator: Ruan, Limin
Zhao, Yanjie
Chen, Zihao
Zeng, Wei
Wang, Siliang
Liang, Dong
Zhao, Jinling
coredata.dc:description: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) quantum dots (QDs) with unique fluorescence (FL) properties are extensively used for sensors, photoelectrochemical devices, energy storage, and hydrogen evolution. The sufficient demand to develop green and environmentally friendly methods has led to the popularization of MoS2 QD preparation. Here, we provided a proper way to prepare MoS2 QDs by virtue of the ethylenediamine (EN)-assisted exfoliation of MoS2 bulk powder. The whole synthesis process was free of high-toxic agents and carried out in an aqueous solution. The resultant MoS2 QDs presented good dispersion in the aqueous solution and the blue emission at 427 nm. The FL quantum yield of 19% in this work was higher than the yields of most reported MoS2 QDs prepared from their bulk powders. EN was successfully bound to the Mo atom based on the XPS analysis, and the entire S atom was at an oxidation state. Meanwhile, the as-prepared MoS2 QDs possessed high-performance air-stable properties and thus proved to be an effective fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ ions with excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and fast response. The limit detection was calculated to be 0.4 μmol/L. The resultant MoS2 QDs can be regarded a potential candidate of fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2 quantum dots
Fe3+ ion
Fluorescent probe
Detector
Fluorescence quantum yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots and nanosheets
Optical properties of as-prepared MoS2 QDs
FL detection of Fe3+ with as-prepared MoS2 QDs in aqueous solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of as-prepared MoS2 QDs detection for Fe3+ ions.

(a) SEM image of the original layered MoS2 powder. (b) SEM image of a typical morphology of MoS2 nanosheets. (c) SEM image of a small MoS2 particle. (d) SEM image in large magnification of MoS2 nanosheets. (e) TEM image of exfoliation after sonication for 3 h. (f) TEM image after the hydrothermal process at 140 °C for 14 h.
(a) AFM image of MoS2 nanosheets. (b) Height profile of different MoS2 nanosheets. (c) Crystalline nature of MoS2 QDs with lattice spacing of 0.27 nm. (d) Statistic of particle size of MoS2 QDs.
XPS spectra of (a) S 2p peak of MoS2 powder, MoS2 nanosheets, and MoS2 QDs, (b) N 1S peak of MoS2 QDs, (c) N 1 s peak of MoS2 nanosheets, and (d) N 1 s peak of MoS2 QDs.

(a) FL spectra with different EN concentrations. (b) Absorbance of samples with different EN concentrations. (c) Digital image of the resultant MoS2 QD suspension with different EN concentrations, and luminescence image of samples excited at 328 nm. (d) UV–vis (black), excitation, and emission spectra (red and blue) of MoS2 QDs. (e) Spectra of the MoS2 QDs solution at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Emission spectrum of the MoS2 QDs/Fe3+ system with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (b) Non-linearity relationship (FL intensity) of the MoS2 QDs/Fe3+ system at 0–500 μmol/L. (c) Schematic of the transformation process for Fe3+ detection. (d) Liner relationship of the MoS2 QDs/Fe3+ system at 0–50 μmol/L. (e) Selectivity of Fe3+ ion sensing at 500 μmol/L concentration with different cations on FL intensity. (f) Cross-interference between Fe3+ and cations.
(a) FL spectra of the MoS2 QDs/Fe3+ system at different time intervals of 1, 2, 3, 5, and 10 mins. (b) Comparison in the enlarged view.

head.ce:title: Ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal method to fabricate MoS2 quantum dots in aqueous solution as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Analysis and Application of Agro-Ecological Big Data, Anhui University, No. 111 Jiulong Road, Hefei 230601, Anhui Province, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Analysis and Application of Agro-Ecological Big Data

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center for Analysis and Application of Agro-Ecological Big Data, Anhui University, No. 111 Jiulong Road, Hefei 230601, Anhui Province, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronics and Information Engineering, Anhui University, No. 111 Jiulong Road, Hefei 230601, Anhui Province, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronics and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronics and Information Engineering, Anhui University, No. 111 Jiulong Road, Hefei 230601, Anhui Province, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ethylenediamine-assistant hydrothermal method to fabricate MoS2 quantum dots in aqueous solution as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ ions detection.

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) quantum dots (QDs) with unique fluorescence (FL) properties are extensively used for sensors, photoelectrochemical devices, energy storage, and hydrogen evolution. The sufficient demand to develop green and environmentally friendly methods has led to the popularization of MoS2 QD preparation. Here, we provided a proper way to prepare MoS2 QDs by virtue of the ethylenediamine (EN)-assisted exfoliation of MoS2 bulk powder. The whole synthesis process was free of high-toxic agents and carried out in an aqueous solution. The resultant MoS2 QDs presented good dispersion in the aqueous solution and the blue emission at 427 nm. The FL quantum yield of 19% in this work was higher than the yields of most reported MoS2 QDs prepared from their bulk powders. EN was successfully bound to the Mo atom based on the XPS analysis, and the entire S atom was at an oxidation state. Meanwhile, the as-prepared MoS2 QDs possessed high-performance air-stable properties and thus proved to be an effective fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ ions with excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and fast response. The limit detection was calculated to be 0.4 μmol/L. The resultant MoS2 QDs can be regarded a potential candidate of fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-like two-dimensional [1–4] materials, especially the transition metal dichalcogenide [5–9], are extensively investigated because of their stable binary compounds, highly anisotropic layered structure, good thermal stability and conductivity, and relatively low toxic effects. These materials possess interesting, promising, and extensive applications, such as in the photoelectrochemical, energy storage, hydrogen evolution, and sensor fields. In particular, the molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) bulk with indirect gap (1.2 eV) [10] possesses good optical semiconductor properties, while the MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) exhibit indirect energy bandgaps at an X-point of the bottom of conduction bands [11,12]. Strong fluorescence (FL) behavior can be observed when the lateral size is less than 10 nm. Two ways are used for MoS2 QD preparation. One approach is the “top–down” method by the direct exfoliation from bulk powders [13,14]. The other approach is the “bottom–up” method [15–18] by assembling atoms or molecules into QDs. The unique structure, particularly the large specific surface area and the weak interaction between MoS2 bulk layers, enables different elementals to be intercalated. Recent continuous efforts have focused on the preparation of MoS2 QDs via “top–down” cutting processes through hydrothermal [19–21], electrochemical [6,13], microwave methodologies [22]. Alkaline metal ions [6,23,24] and some organic solvents, and even toxic chemicals, are widely used to obtain QDs. Among them, N-methyl-pyrrolidone [7], alkali metal ion of sodium [24], and n-butyl lithium [25] have been confirmed to intercalate successfully into layers. However, as-synthesized MoS2 QDs from aqueous solutions are inefficient due to their low-yield surfactant-assisted exfoliations [24]. In some studies, the phase transition from 2H to 1 T phase occur with high intercalation concentration [26,27]. In the case of electrochemical treatments, high Li contents promote 2H to 1 T phase transition and result in a relatively small lateral size. Despite the remarkable achievements, certain insufficiencies related to low fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) and high toxic reagent need to be improved. For most of the produced MoS2 QDs synthesized from their bulk powders, the FLQY are less than 8% [1], and the toxic reagents subsequently threaten the people′s health and even pollutes the environment.

Recent studies on MoS2 QDs as FL detection probes have been already extended to biosensing [28–32], gas sensing [33], and chemical sensing applications [4,34–39]. As one of the most important metal ions, ferric ion plays a significant role in environmental science, chemical industry, and personal health. Increasing attention [35,40–45] has focused on the exploration of the FL sensor system of Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and fast rapid response. Most Fe3+ ionic compounds have good water solubility. Consequently, the design and development of Fe3+ ion detection adopted for aqueous solution have becoming increasingly meaningful for practical applications. FL response behavior in the presence of detection can be divided into “turn-on” and “turn-off” detection systems. Nevertheless, Fe3+ ion is well-known as an FL quencher due to its paramagnetic nature [23]. In general, MoS2 QDs FL probes based on “turn-off” detection systems are studied in the accurate and convenient application of Fe3+ ion detection [38,42]. The design and development of probes with highly selective, sensitive, and environmentally friendly Fe3+ ion detection have become increasingly apparent, and they subsequently emerged as a fundamental concern.

Here, the prepared MoS2 QDs can be obtained in presence of ethylenediamine (EN) by a “top-down” approach. By virtue of EN assisted exfoliation with MoS2 bulk powder, the resulted MoS2 QDs possessed the high FLQY of 19% which is much higher than most of reported MoS2 QDs synthesized by its powder source [1]. As-prepared MoS2 QDs also proposed a sensitive fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ ion. The whole process of MoS2 QDs’ synthesis was free of toxic reagents. Consequently, a sensitive and facile detection method based on the FL of the resultant MoS2 QDs was established.

High-purity MoS2 bulk powders (99.99%, Alfa Aesar), anhydrous EN (99%), and hydrogen nitrate (HCl, 38% in water) were derived from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, KCl, AlCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, NaCl, ZnSO4, Co(NO3)2·H2O, MnSO4·7H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, and CuSO4·3H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots and nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Preparing MoS2 QDs in presence of EN: MoS2 bulk powder (0.1 g) was added to 10 mL of ultrapure water. The pH values of the solutions were adjusted to 12 with different ENs (low toxicity), from 0.5 to 3 mL. Then, the mixtures were stirred for 12 h and sonicated for 3 h. The mixtures were subsequently transferred into a 20 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 140 °C for 14 h. After the solutions were cooled to room temperature, the supernatants were collected and centrifuged for 30 min at the speed of 9000 rpm.

Purification procedure: A purification procedure was carried out to remove EN. First, the excessive EN was neutralized by HCl acid to allow the reaction with EN dihydrochloride (free of toxicity). Then, the solutions were further concentrated by a rotary evaporator, and only the MoS2 QDs were collected using alcohol. Most of EN dihydrochloride was separated from the solutions. After repeating the procedure thrice, the final concentrated MoS2 QDs were dispersed into 10 mL of water and stored in darkness at 5 °C.

Preparing MoS2 QDs in presence of NaOH: 0.1 g 2 bulk powder was added to 10 mL of ultrapure water. The pH values of the solutions were adjusted to 12 with NaOH (2 mol/L) of 2 mL. The mixtures were stirred for 12 h and sonicated for 3 h, and the mixtures were subsequently transferred into a 20 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 140 °C for 14 h. The supernatants were collected and centrifuged for 30 min at the speed of 9000 rpm. Then supernatant was purified by using a 1000D dialysis bag for 24 h and stored in darkness at 5 °C.

MoS2 nanosheets: The supernatants were centrifuged for 15 min at the speed of 3500 rpm. And the precipitation washed by water for three times and alcohol for three times. Then the precipitation dried at 60 °C.

The solutions with different concentrations of metal ions were prepared as aqueous solutions. The Fe3+ ion solutions with different concentrations from 0 to 500 μmol/L were applied for detection. An MoS2 QDs solution (200 μL) was added to the above solutions and sloshed for mixture uniformity. Then, an FL detection for the mixture was carried out immediately by a UV spectrophotometer. Selective experiments of various metal ions were operated with the same concentration of 500 μmol/L. The FL intensities were recorded for the same conditions.

The field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image was taken by S-4800 (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed with JEM-2100 (JEM, Japan). Atom force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed on a Bruker NanoScope V instrument in tapping mode. All FL spectra were collected at room temperature on an F4500 FL spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The UV–vis spectra were recorded with a UV-2910 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature. The detection experiments were characterized by the FL spectrum with excitation of 340 nm. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected on a Siemens D-5005 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). FLQY was measured according to a reported method [18] in which a reference quinine sulfate was considered by virtue of the absorbency values and integrated FL intensity. The integral FL intensity and the absorbance were measured. The calculation equation of FLQY is shown as follows:
(1)
Y M
=
Y q
×
A q
A M
×
F M
F q
where Y M and Y q are their FLQY of as-prepared MoS2 QDs and quinine sulfate, A M and A q are the absorbance intensity, and F M and F q are the FL intensity.

A summary of the MoS2 QDs synthetic procedure is presented in Fig. 1 . MoS2 QDs are prepared by the EN-assisted hydrothermal method. MoS2 QDs are directly prepared in an aqueous solution, and they show excellent aqueous dispersibility and strong luminescence. Exfoliation in aqueous solutions are reportedly ineffective in general for MoS2 nanosheet fabrication, and this constraint may be caused by the high surface tension [24]. The EN, as a low-toxic alkaline, in this study is applied to the solution to assist in the exfoliation. The van der Waals radius of EN is 3.4 Å, and the radius of Na+ is 0.97 Å. EN molecules can be introduced into the inner layers because the interplanar crystal spacing along the (0 0 2) of the MoS2 bulk powders is calculated to be 6.1470 Å. In addition, EN provides amino functional species for MoS2 QDs fabrication.

The morphology and characterization of functional groups are determined by SEM and TEM. The SEM image of the original MoS2 bulk powder, as a typical layered structural material, is shown in Fig. 2 a. According to previous studies, bulk powders can be easily exfoliated into layered nanosheets with several micrometers by sonication [7,9,46]. However, another previous study [24] proved that exfoliation from aqueous solutions is difficult when MoS2 nanosheets are used. Some organic agents [47–49], which are beneficial for surface tension reduction in aqueous solutions [24], may assist in the exfoliation into nanosheets. Based on our study, the original size of MoS2 powder was several micrometers. After the sonication, some of these powders were split into small particles with several hundred nanometers. When the thicknesses of these particles reached less than several dozens of nanometers, the flat MoS2 nanosheets could be observed and continued to be cut into the MoS2 QDs in the hydrothermal procedure. Large-diameter MoS2 nanosheets are observed on the basis of SEM analysis (Fig. 2b). The images show nanoscale representations of the as-exfoliated MoS2 nanosheets, in which the majority of the plane sizes are 1–4 µm (Fig. 2d) in the initial stage. Our prepared MoS2 nanosheets approaches a rectangle morphology as opposed to the other reported MoS2 nanosheets [1,50,51]. Large magnifications of the MoS2 nanosheets are shown in Fig. 2c and 2d. The MoS2 powder prepared in different synthesis stages are all in the 2H phases, as verified by XRD analysis (Fig. S1) and the TEM images (Fig. 2e and 2f). Some small nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 2c, have diameters of less than 200 nm, and they can be also found in the TEM image (Fig. 2f). On the basis of XRD analysis, the diffraction peak of (0 0 2) is shifted slightly to the relatively low angle. This scheme indicates an increase in values in interplanar spacing. Some EN molecules are likely intercalated into the inner layer of the MoS2 bulk powders.

AFM analysis was performed to investigate the morphology of the MoS2 nanosheets. Some tiny holes of about several nanometers are evidently distributed on the surface of these sheets. The thicknesses of the nanosheets range from dozens of nanometers to over a hundred nanometers (Fig. 3 a and 3b). This finding suggests that small nanosheets peel off from the surface of the large ones. On the basis of the AFM analysis, we suggest that the lateral size of exfoliation from the bulk powder probably changes from a large size to a small size. The diameter of these nanosheets is extremely small at only several micrometers (Fig. S2). The particles can be considered QDs only if their diameters are less than 10 nm. A strong photoluminescence can be observed due to the quantum-size effect. The high-crystalline nature of the MoS2 QDs with a lattice spacing of 0.27 nm for the (1 0 0) faces of the MoS2 crystals can be observed from high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3c), which is in consistent with that in a previous report [1]. The results of the TEM analysis confirm that the MoS2 QDs with uniformly distributed (Fig. 3d) are synthesized, and they have obtained a crystalline structure and achieved good dispersion in the aqueous solution. MoS2 QDs with the diameter of 1–10 nm was statistized in Fig. 3d. It was clearly shown that a continually distributed size could be observed with the average size of 6.2 nm.

The surface groups and compositions of the resultant MoS2 nanosheets and the MoS2 QDs are investigated using the XPS spectra. The parameters are highly sensitive in the chemical state. Mo and S are further confirmed using the high-resolution XPS spectra, respectively. The spectra of Mo 3d regions were shown in Fig. 4 a. The peaks around 228.8 and 232.0 eV of the original MoS2 bulk powder corresponded to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 [18,52], which belonged to the 2H phase of MoS2. In particular, the Mo 3d spectrum of as-prepared MoS2 nanosheets showed two typical peaks at 229.7 and 232.8 eV corresponding to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2. The binding energy of as-prepared MoS2 nanosheet is highly consistent with the previous study [24], and the reported value of the Mo 3d spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets is 229.3 and 232.5 eV, respectively. The Mo 3d spectrum of the as-prepared MoS2 QDs showed two peaks located at 230.4 eV (Mo 3d5/2) and 233.5 eV (Mo 3d3/2). In comparison with MoS2 powder and nanosheet, the two peaks of Mo 3d spectrum had apparently shifted to the higher binding energy due to the electron transfer between the MoS2 and another atom [53]. In this study, we preferred to believe that N atom formed the Mo-N bond [54]. All of the Mo 3d spectra of MoS2 powders, nanosheets and the MoS2 QDs may suggest that the dominance of Mo is the 4 + oxidation state.

The spectra of Mo 3d regions were shown in Fig. 4b. The peaks at 161.5 and 162.7 eV were observed for the S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, which represent the 2H-phase of the MoS2 powder. The S 2p peaks around 162.5 and 163.7 eV are attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals of divalent sulfide ions [54] for the MoS2 nanosheets. The peaks around 166.7 and 167.7 eV are ascribed to the + 4 oxidation state of S, which are possibly attributed to the SO3 2− group and likely formed by oxidation due to the locally high temperature in the experimental (Fig. 1). S atoms are all at their oxidation states, revealing that the whole reaction is affected by oxygen. This result suggests that EN increases the sensitivity of crystals to surface deterioration. However, oxidized QDs remain to have a high FLQY. In general, QDs may have FL intensity decay when exposed to air. On the contrary, a slight decline (Fig. S3) in FL spectra intensity after three months of storage in air is achieved, and this phenomenon indicates good FL stability in relation to oxygen in air.

The peak at 399.4 eV, which can be ascribed to N 1 s peak [17,54] (Fig. 4c and 4d), is larger than the peak at 398.5 eV (free EN) and suggests that N atoms are successfully doped into MoS2 QDs matrix. Carbon, hydrogen, and other nitrogen atoms extend to the reverse of the Mo atoms. According to another study [55], the peak of 400.3 eV can also be ascribed into Mo-N bond of MoS2 nanosheets. The different Mo-N peak value between MoS2 nanosheets and QDs may be caused by different surface edge [53]. In addition, S 2p peaks (Fig. S4) are slightly increased after storage in air for 10 months, indicating that the S atom is prone to high-binding energy in their oxidation state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of as-prepared MoS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The influences of EN on the as-synthesized MoS2 QDs are further explored by analyzing the FL and UV-absorbance spectra. No FL emission is observed for the solution without EN, and an ultrasonication with EN is carried out for 3 h. These results imply that EN, as the assistant solvent, plays an important role in MoS2 QD preparation. The influences of the as-prepared solutions with different EN concentrations on the FL spectra are subsequently investigated. Obvious peaks are observed around 408–480 nm of the FL emitting spectra (Fig. 5 a) for the case of different EN concentrations. With the rise in concentration of EN, the peak of the FL spectra exhibits a large red shift from 408 to 480 nm, and the intensity of the emitting spectra with different EN concentrations show dependence on luminescence quenching. The difference in the emission peaks between the 0.5- and 3-mL samples is 72 nm. This result reveals an apparent red shift in the FL emission of MoS2 QDs with the increase in concentration of EN. The characteristic absorption spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 5b. The peaks at less than 300 nm can be ascribed to EN absorption, as depicted by the slight red shift from 270 to 280 nm. The obtained absorbance intensity is reflected in general by the solvent concentration. The regular increase in absorption spectrum corresponds to the increase in EN sorption, indicating the increased concentration of EN in the solutions. Compared with the peaks of the absorbance spectrum of pure EN, the new peaks located above 300 emerge together with the absorbance of MoS2 QDs in the aqueous solutions of EN. This finding indicates that all of the absorbance peaks have significant red shifts with the increase in EN concentration. The absorbance intensities of the solutions above 300 nm are also enhanced, and the UV-absorption spectra of these solutions exhibit broad absorption. The large red shifts in the absorption spectra lead to large red shifts in the FL spectra. The colors of the resultant MoS2 QD suspensions shift to yellow (Fig. 5c), suggesting that the concentration of the MoS2 QDs is also increased. At the meanwhile, their FL spectrum have apparently red shift (Fig. 5c). The contact angle of the resultant MoS2 QD suspension (Fig. S5) with different EN concentrations are increased gradually. This result also reveals that the concentrations of the as-prepared MoS2 QD suspension have increased. Despite the significantly high absorption intensity, the FL spectra decline gradually with the increase in EN concentration (Fig. 5a).

MoS2 QDs are purified for application in Fe3+ detection. The FL spectra of the obtained MoS2 QDs exhibit strong emission at 427 nm (Fig. 5d) under the excitation of 340 nm. The full width at the half maximum of the MoS2 QDs is approximately 110 nm. The typical absorption peak of the MoS2 QDs is located at 250 nm, which is different from that of the EN solution. The as-prepared MoS2 QDs solution has an interesting variant light emitting property. Fig. 5e shows the PL spectra of the MoS2 QDs solution added with EN excited under different wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm. The emission spectra of the MoS2 QDs exhibit an excitation dependent feature in the wide range due to the polydispersity in the lateral dimension range [56]. The intensities of the dominant peaks of FL spectra depend on the excitation wavelengths and show a gradual decline with the increasing of excitation wavelengths. The continued red shift in FL spectra due to the increasing wavelength indicates a heterogeneously distributed MoS2 QDs and surface states. With the increase in excitation wavelength, the emission band covers the visible wavelength range of 427–476 nm under different excitations. The FLQY of the prepared MoS2 QDs is measured to be 19%. In many cases, the FLQY of the MoS2 QDs prepared by exfoliation is less than 8% [1]. In addition, the MoS2 QDs fabricated by Na+ intercalation is also synthesized (Fig. S6). Here, 2 mL of the 2 mol/L aqueous NaOH is used to prepare MoS2 QDs in the same procedure as that of EN. The color of the NaOH suspension is significantly different from that of the EN suspension (Fig. S7), and the EN suspension appears to be more yellow, indicating a highly efficient MoS2 QD preparation. The FLQY of the MoS2 QDs fabricated by NaOH is 6%. The FLQY of as-synthesized MoS2 QDs in presence of EN is much higher than that of NaOH. The possible reason can be ascribed into Mo-N bond. In the case of N-doped MoS2 [17], the doped N atoms are beneficial in improving electric conductivity. As for the MoS2 QDs, the EN can be used as a photogenerated electron donor, and it can be subsequently transferred into the MoS2 QDs [57]. Therefore, unlike in those made by NaOH, as-synthesized MoS2 QDs can achieve a much larger FLQY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL detection of Fe3+ with as-prepared MoS2 QDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The FL probe of the Fe3+ ions is achieved on the basis of the Fe3+ ions used to quench the luminescence of the MoS2 QDs. All detections are performed in an aqueous solution at room temperature, and the excitation wavelength is set to 340 nm for all measurements. The different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, from 0 to 500 μmol/L, are considered. All measurements are carried out within 1 min after adding MoS2 QDs to the different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. FL quenching is observed during mixing (Fig. 6 a), indicating fast response. The relative FL intensity of (F 0–F)/F 0 is used as a contrast versus the concentration of Fe3+ ions, where F and F0 are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively. When the maximum concentration of the Fe3+ ions is increased to 500 μmol/L, the FL is almost entirely quenched. Meanwhile, with the increase in concentration of Fe3+ ions, the FL intensity continuously decreases, but the rate slows down eventually. A non-linearity occurs when the detected concentration of Fe3+ ions is in the relatively large scale. The FL quenching can be effectively described in the exponential function (Fig. 6b).

A simple model is schematically proposed, as shown in Fig. 6c, to explain the inner process based on the above studies. According to the XPS analysis, all sulphure atoms of QDs are in the oxidation state. We suggest that the oxygen atom is on the surface of the sulphure atom. When dispersed in the aqueous solution, the sulphure atom is almost completely transformed into SO3 2-. Then, a compact layer is formed on the surface of the QDs, and the PL peak and intensity are stabilized. Given that EN is successfully bound to the Mo atom, the resultant MoS2 QDs have a high FLQY of 19%. When Fe3+ is added to the aqueous solution of QDs, the oxygen atom acts with Fe3+. The decline in FL indicates that the FL of the QDs is quenched by Fe3+. This finding is considerably different from those presented in a previous report about the FL probe of Fe3+ [40]. Conversely, oxidized QDs may retain high FLQY and provide stable FL properties for long-term storage in air.

In the low-range concentration of Fe3+ ions, the value of (F 0–F)/F 0 is linearly correlated with the concentration of the hypochlorite in range of 5–50 μmol/L. The linear relationship can be further achieved (Fig. 6d) as follows:
F -
F 0
/
F 0
= 0.9906 - 0.0038 c
, where c is the concentration of the Fe3+ ions. The linearity can reach as high as 99.71%, indicating good linearity in the low concentration. The calculated detection limit is 0.4 μmol/L by using 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank solution, and s is the slope of the calibration curve. This result is better than the reported value by using 1 μmol/L of MoS2 QDs [35].

The different cations for the FL intensity of the MoS2 QDs are measured with the same procedure, including the use of Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Fe 2+ ions. The concentration of these cations is 500 μmol/L. As shown in Fig. 6e, only Fe3+ ions can induce a relatively high FL response, which suggests that MoS2 QDs are responsive to Fe3+ ions with good selectivity. The results further show that metal ions hardly affect the FL intensity of the MoS2 QDs, suggesting that MoS2 QDs are suitable for Fe3+ ion detection. To study the cross-interference on FL intensity between Fe3+ and other cations, the Fe3+ concentration of 200 μmol/L was added into the different cations on fluorescence exploration, including the cations of Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Fe 2+. The concentration of these cations is 500 μmol/L (Fig. 6f). As shown in Fig. 6f, when Fe3+ ions and other cations were mixed with MoS2 QDs, the declined FL intensity indicated that Fe3+ had a strong quenching effect. The results further show that those metal ions hardly affect the FL intensity of MoS2 QDs, suggesting that as-prepared MoS2 QDs are suitable for Fe3+ ion detection.

The time dependence of our approach on Fe3+ ion detection in aqueous samples is investigated by selecting different time intervals to detect shifts in FL intensities. The time intervals between the exposure to MoS2 QDs and the measurements are set to 1, 2, 5, and 10 min to simulate the different measurement conditions (Fig. 7 a). A deviation can be usually found due to the time delay or artificial errors in a fast detection. The FL intensity between 1 min and 10 mins showed a 2.34% decline (Fig. 7b) when Fe3+ exposed to MoS2 QDs within 10 mins. The results indicate that the FL intensity for the solutions with Fe3+ ions on MoS2 QDs exhibit time-varying dependence.

The high water-solubility and sensitivity of the prepared MoS2 QDs are favorable for FL-based applications of Fe3+ ions. All experiments are carried out in aqueous solutions without the use of toxic agents. Our studies show that FL intensity for the solution of Fe3+ ions on MoS2 QDs exhibit time-varying dependence. After further analyses and experiments, we suggest two possible usages of EN in the whole procedure. One approach is the intercalation of EN in the interlayer space of the MoS2 bulk powder as a means to improve efficiency for the exfoliation of MoS2 nanosheets in the aqueous solutions. The other approach is to significantly improve the FLQY of the MoS2 QDs. Its unique emission feature makes it promising for Fe3+ ion detection. On the basis of the XPS analysis, EN is successfully bound to the Mo atom, and the entire S atom is in the oxidation state. However, oxidized QDs can still work as an FL probe for Fe3+ detection while maintaining high FLQY. This approach also possesses a high-performance air-stable property. The demonstrated technique can be applied to the syntheses of other QDs for application in FL probe, and it can maintain high FLQY.

Limin Ruan: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yanjie Zhao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zihao Chen: Data curation. Wei Zeng: Investigation, Supervision. Siliang Wang: Validation. Dong Liang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Jinling Zhao: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1808085MF185), China; National Natural Science Foundation of China (11704002); Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2017B182), China; Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1908085QF251), China; Support Project of Outstanding Young Talents in Anhui Provincial Universities (gxyqZD2018006), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.146811.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.C. Wang, J.Z. Ou, S. Balendhran, A.F. Chrimes, M. Mortazavi, D.D. Yao, M.R. Field, K. Latham, V. Bansal, J.R. Friend, S. Zhuiykov, N.V. Medhekar, M.S. Strano, K. Kalantar-zadeh, Electrochemical control of photoluminescence in two-dimensional MoS2 nanoflakes, ACS Nano 7 (2013) 10083–10093.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic wastewater purification with simultaneous hydrogen production using MoS2 QD-decorated hierarchical assembly of ZnIn2S4 on reduced graphene oxide photocatalyst
Water Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Q. Zhang, L.L. Wang, C.B. Liu, J.M. Luo, J. Crittenden, X. Liu, T. Cai, J.L. Yuan, Y. Pei, Y.T. Liu, Photocatalytic wastewater purification with simultaneous hydrogen production using MoS2 QD-decorated hierarchical assembly of ZnIn2S4 on reduced graphene oxide photocatalyst, Water Res. 121 (2017) 11–19.
